transcript.text: Hey, afternoon.
Hey, Rob.
Hey, guys.
Hey, how's it going?
Good, good.
Thanks for taking the call.
Of course.
Hey, Chris.
You're on mute.
How are you guys?
Yeah, we're good.
How are you guys?
Awesome.
Yeah, really good.
Thanks for taking the time.
We've heard some good things about you guys and thought it made sense for us to chat.
Janice spoke highly of you, and we've seen that you've helped out some businesses that I think are similar to ours.
So curious to learn a little bit more.
Why don't we start with intros, and then we'll see where the conversation goes.
Does that make sense?
Yeah, sounds like a great approach.
Appreciate you all reaching out, and love to hear you're hearing good things.
So yeah, quick intro, Kyle Gaudreau, VP of Customer Growth over here, based in Oakland, California, but now in our brand spanking new SF office.
It's my second day here.
Exciting times.
A little bit about me, what my role here is essentially amongst a few things, essentially leading on the sales side.
Not your typical salesperson.
Previous to this, I was leading all the client strategy services side.
From GrowthX, joined back in October.
Previous to that, was actually signed up to be the first GrowthX customer.
I have a long career in growth, 19 years agency side in-house.
A lot of my specialty lies in brands that need to diversify beyond paid search.
Organic usually being a key part of that amongst standing up other paid channels.
Most recent stop was at a company called Homebase, which actually, I believe you folks are up in Canada, or at least, Chris, you are.
Almost my entire team was up in Toronto.
Oh, nice.
So I got to know that place very well.
Amazing city.
But anyways, yeah, I was leading growth at Homebase.
That's where I came across Marcel about a year ago when he was just sharing some cool things he was doing at DeepGram and the rest of his history.
Begged him to help us out and build some workflows and, you know, so excited at the opportunity I had to join myself.
Awesome.
Bruno, you want to do a quick one on your end?
Yeah, man.
I'm Bruno Santilli here.
I'm Marcel's brother.
I've been keeping up with my brother's journey for a while, right?
He went the marketing route.
I went the sales route.
I was actually a personal trainer before I started my sales career and got into this industrial segment B2B and honestly got burned out last year.
know, I just wanted to unplug and go down to South America and just take a year off.
And then my brother goes, hey, why don't you come work for us instead?
And I'm like, yes, that's a great idea.
I have lots to learn.
So I'm here learning a lot and watching our growth.
From three to the moon, hopefully.
Cool.
Appreciate that.
Yeah, I would love to meet you.
Yeah, Rob, why don't you go first?
Yeah, so founder and CEO of VerticalScope.
Really built the business from the ground up.
I've been through lots of different kind of iterations of the business and the company.
Always exciting kind of being in this space.
We, you know, for us, we are particularly fond of kind of like user-generating content and particularly a lot of our forum communities.
But we've also got a lot of professional-generating content.
So a really good mix across the board.
Generally, like, I think enthusiast communities is kind of where our heart has been and around the kind of the authenticity that those have and the knowledge that's shared in those communities.
So it's been almost 20 years for me and looking to kind of likewise, you know, see what we can do.
Moving forward with AI.
Very cool.
Appreciate that.
Yeah.
And Chris, CEO here, been involved with the business for about 10 years and inside working in the company for, oh my gosh, I think we're coming up on eight years.
In beginning of June, which is very soon.
Anyway, so, you know, had a chance to work with Rob and helping build the company up with a lot of M&A.
So M&A has been a big focus of mine, but also I've spent a good portion of my career around digital advertising.
So everything from direct programmatic to affiliate marketing and kind of everything else.
So I spend a decent amount of my time on monetization as well and just sort of general BD, all kinds of stuff.

So, you know, our interests, our interests with you guys is we've seen you, what we gather from the outside is you've had a lot of success helping increase content generation or accelerate content generation.
And that's one angle for us.
So, you know, we're thinking about many different ways of leveraging AI for content generation, but not just content generation, but other types of use cases like on the forum.
So things like content summarization, we've got a successful translation experiment going on right now that's scaling.

But one of the things we're curious about, Rob weigh in here as well, is, you know, are there things that you've seen, things you've supported that can really help increase the production of content, particularly in a user-generated content setting?
So really increase the velocity, more posting, higher quality posts, you know, posts that have proper titles that are more, you know, will rank better in search, that kind of stuff.
That's, I think, a curiosity of ours.
You know, could we leverage?
You know, AI or capabilities like you guys would have to increase content velocity.
Is that, Rob, sum it up?

Yeah, I think the only thing I'd add there is just, you know, the challenge for forums over the years has been, particularly post-COVID, has been kind of, you know, increased competition for user attention and eyeballs.
And how do you get people off of TikTok coming to the forums and, you know, kind of digging in?
you know, Google algorithms have been both friendly and unfriendly over the past number of years.
I think, you know, one of the things we think a lot about is just, you know, how can we become less dependent on Google algorithms?
But one of the things that Google has been seemingly rewarding is around kind of expert content, but also recency.
There seems to be this huge kind of new push around, like, just, like, the date and the amount of activity.
So we've done a bunch of.
experiments internally with, you know, using some of our own kind of homebrew AI solutions to try and increase, but, and it's worked, which is great.
I think, you know, one of the things we're thinking about is like, how could we actually scale this in a meaningful way?
And, you know, knowing that it does work for us, like, what could the future look like?
So that's kind of what we're thinking about.
Super helpful context.

A couple of follow-up questions to that, you know, so, of course, doing a bit of research before our conversation, just trying to get my head around, you know, the sites that you operate, as you think about a potential engagement, is there a particular area of sites where you feel you have, you would want to prioritize something like this, and use that as almost like a test case that expands?
And, you know, happy to share a little bit more about how we do, and maybe that informs that to a degree, but I'm just curious if there's like a, an industry that is most

I would say largely it's our automotive forums that are probably the most interesting kind of test case where we've had some early successes and where the traffic is both kind of valuable enough, but also the posting volumes are kind of, I don't want to call it dwindling, but there's small year up year declines that are unwelcome.
So, yeah, that's kind of the area that, at least for me, kind of jumps out as like, that'd be a really nice place if we could win there.
Got it.
Got it.
So, yeah, more visibility, whether that be search engines, AI search, helping drive more engagement within the forums.
I would imagine there's a monetization component of being able to then drive that engagement.
And you mentioned something around quality traffic.
I'm just curious to understand that a little bit more, how you define that.
Yeah, I think of it as like...
like...
How do we get more engagement?
How do we get the revisits?
How do we get the users, instead of coming back once a week or once a month, coming back daily?

A lot of that has to do with, especially as you get into the really niche communities, it's like, is there enough content to do daily newsletters or is there enough content to get people to come back and actually spend a meaningful amount of time?
Got it.
Got it.
Helpful.
Okay, cool.
Yeah, well, so happy to share, you know, how we operate here.
You know, we can keep this, you know, conversational, you know, as I share a bit, feel free to stop me, you know, ask questions along the way.
And, you know, we can just take it from there.
You know, I know we only have 30 or 20 minutes left here.
So let me just jump into it, set some context of how we operate here and just a little bit about our journey.
Oh, sorry.
One second.
Had all these IT updates I had to do to my computer and now I have all.
These approvals I have to do for every little thing.
Here we go.
You guys see my screen?
Yep.
Yeah, cool.
Just a little bit about our journey and how this all came about.
So as I mentioned, you know, I signed up to be the first Fathom's customer.

And, you know, in the early days, what really resonated with me when I met Marcel just a little over a year ago now was just a lot of the shared experience of having built content engines at multiple stops.
You know, here, Marcel at Scale AI, Service Titan, et cetera.
And just seeing the pain that can come with that, but also the fruits of that labor when you get it right.
You know, this is a little bit more tailored to his journey, but I've seen a lot of similar in my own stops.
You know, Atre, Sendoso, Anaplan, most recently Homebase, where there's just so much time and effort that goes into building these teams.
You know, you then need to coordinate with those teams on developing the right topics, and you need to figure out your editorial process and ensuring that you're putting out high quality content.
And you need to figure out the things that help support the user experience thereof, whether that be the templates, images, the staging and publishing of that content.
There's just so many friction points along the way that being organic, being a long game, those friction points are so, so key.

And so him and I, what resonated with me early on in hearing his story was he took a little bit of a different approach that I really appreciated, thinking of some of these websites as publications and building out teams as if you are kind of running almost a newsroom with managing editors, a set of writers, and really developing a very comprehensive process thereof.

But what really brought me to GrowthX and a lot of our customers today was just about a year ago where Marcel was sharing a lot of success at DeepGram, where taking a lot of those learnings of scaling those content programs, but starting to think about maybe there's a difference.
A way of approaching this.
Maybe we can start to break down the jobs to be done of someone developing content into a variety of different workflows.
And the idea here was not just automating everything possible, pushing buttons, and then everything just takes care of itself.
It's more how do you arm these experts with richer contacts faster and take things that used to take hours, days, and give it that to them in minutes.
And then what can you then produce as a result of that?
You know, he certainly felt like he was onto something here, as the data shows here at DeepGram.
You know, just insane traffic growth, huge increase in closed-won deals.

And I think just one of the biggest testaments to that this was real true impact was the lead investor for DeepGram is the lead investor for our Series A that we're going to be announcing over the next week.
I feel like I've been saying that over the next week, for a few weeks now, hopefully it is actually the next week.
But, you know, these things, you know, you want to have the big bang and get.
Right.
So, you know, Marcel is someone who loves to teach, loves to share, started doing these workshops last year.
This is where I attended.
And, you know, one of the key takeaways from the workshop, especially for myself, was like, this is amazing.
This is building on some really cool things I had done to automate content at fast stops.
Now bring in LLMs.
Man, the possibilities are endless.
The reality was, like, I don't have a ton of time to build these workflows.
My team didn't have a ton of time to build these workflows.
And there was just this need for folks to both have the support on developing the custom workflows, as well as that strategic layer of what are those workflows actually, what is the purpose?
What are we executing and why?
And that's where VerticalScope just first started coming thing.
We've been fortunate to bring on a lot of amazing brands, the Rams, the Reddits.
You know, a lot of our specialty tends to be in B2B SaaS typically, but, you know, continuing to diversify there and seeing a lot of great impact early on.
And in terms of...
You know, where we see ourselves and what we can do.

This has been an interesting, it's been an interesting, you know, as any company that is in the early phases and trying to prioritize and build something great, we saw no shortage of opportunities of where we could go in the future, what we could do across the growth funnel with our workflows.
That certainly for me, when I signed up was one of the, while I was excited about the SEO impact, there was a lot of things beyond SEO.
I saw opportunities and still do today.
But we need a place to start.
We need to ensure we're doing things at a high level.
And for us, that foundation is starting with content.
For our engagements, what that looks like is that starts in one of two places, editorial or programmatic content here.

And, you know, some of our customers will work with us for both, some one or the other, you know, certainly more tend to skew the editorial side, but we're seeing more and more growing demand on the programmatic side, just in case you're not familiar.

A quick definition on those two things, editorial, typically meaning longer form content, more editorialized content that's going to have a heavier hand from a managing editor and really ensuring you're nailing voice tone, et cetera.
Whereas programmatic, that tends to be highly repeatable content that tends to be templatized.
You're following some sort of consistent pattern and scaling the content to reach those opportunities.
And so that while, of course, there's human and loop steps there, it tends to be less edits, more of a scaling type of challenge that you're solving for there.
And our mindset here is starting with content that allows us to start to build those early signals, start to learn from those signals, continue to adapt, cut, double down, et cetera.
That opens up opportunity to start to broaden distribution for our accounts, whether that be bringing in social media, video, emails, et cetera.
And then we can start to think about, okay.
We have these signals.
How do we help you ensure that this is actually quality?
This is converting.
For a lot of our customers, that tends to be things like sales, signups, demos, etc.
And we can build in things like lead magnets, whatever it may be that suits that brand.
And our point being here is, well, we can do a lot to just increase traffic and do everything possible to index for the highest quality of traffic.

You know, at the end of the day, typically you have to do a little bit more to continue to engage and pull users down and ensure that there's a little bit more foundational elements that surround it to ensure that engagement as they hit your website continues to happen.
That's a bit of a high level.
I'm happy to go through and share a little bit of like how we structure our engagements, a little bit what the outputs look like, what the workflow looks like.
But, you know, before I do that, I'm just curious, you know, any initial thoughts, questions, you know, is.
Is this in the relative ballpark of how you were thinking about us, or is this a little bit different?
A little bit, I guess.
The one, and I don't want you to speak necessarily, but divulge anything confidential about a client.
Sure.
You know, Reddit peaks our interest just in the vantage point of, you know, it's obviously a giant community, UGC, that kind of thing.
You know, your work with Reddit, is it primarily supporting kind of their B2B outreach as far as like their ad sales, or are there integrations you've done with the actual Reddit platform?
Yeah, correct.
It is more the former.
So the team we work with is particularly focused on how do they drive more folks that advertise on their platform.

So a lot of the content that we are creating, similar in terms of industries that you guys have in terms of your forums, but just a little bit of a different objective of what we're actually trying to do with that content.
It is more, you know.
It's really ranging the funnel.

You know, some of it is educational based, you know, trying to capture those and, you know, who may own dealerships and thinking about how to market themselves, et cetera, but a bit of a different flavor.
And have you thought kind of through what, you know, kind of content generation in a more like user-generated content forum could look like?
Is that something that, you know, is within your wheelhouse and capabilities?
Yeah, for full transparency, it is certainly would be a new challenge for us in a variety of ways.
Certainly have explored UGC for a variety of our clients of just like where can we create those loops and help that continue to feed in and help us compound what we're doing for them organically.
For example, I know this is a very different space, but just one timely that's coming to mind.
We're working with the stacklets.
Team, which are the developers of Bolt.new, one of the big competitors to Lovable.
If you've seen Vibe coding around in LinkedIn, that's where these spaces play.
I'm seeing Vibe coding way too often for my taste, but, you know, it is what it is.
And, you know, essentially what we're doing for them is starting to create a set of different prompts and structure that allows us to create pages dynamically as they continue to generate users.

And that allows us to start to scale out all these individual pages and apps, if you will, that will naturally grow as we continue to develop and gain more traction organically and more people engaged.
That just continues to feed the engine of creating more pages, user-generated in this case, that helps us start to, like, really build those flywheels and effects.
Quite different here, you know, leveraging a tool like that.
That is a highly developer-centric.
Literally, our CTO is the one building that for them.
But more sharing.
We never had done that before.
It is something where we're like, okay, let's understand your problem.

We have a set of principal engineers, an amazing CTO, and Daniel, who can understand the problems at a deep level and say, okay, based off of our approach, our first principal's approach to how we think about workflows, how would we adapt and build something that helps you fill your objectives?
So we just want to be super transparent and this would be something that would be highly custom for us.
And that doesn't mean we can't do it, of course, but it is not like we would be coming with this playbook from day one of like, here's how we did this and let's run.
Yeah, and maybe likewise, just in terms of, I think you mentioned the next part you were going to talk about was, you know, how you work in these engagements.
So that might be a good, given that this would be somewhat custom, a good prelude into that.
Yeah, for sure.
I'm happy to show, I think in some ways this, this will be relevant.
There's probably room where we need to adapt this approach to a degree, but we're big on documentation here.
This is just a little bit of a high level.
You know, we won't have time to go through this entire talk here, but just looking at a little bit of the structure of how we break down our process.
As with any engagement, there's a kickoff process.
There's a pre-kickoff process.

And through there, we actually really like to lean on our, you know, our culture of automation, our ability to leverage workflows to inform ourselves as much as possible going into a kickoff and starting with an initial hypothesis.

Oftentimes that has started to be crafted through these sales conversations, and then we can continue that conversation into actually starting to structure the engagement, essentially just trying to find opportunities to move faster.
Through there, we leverage a variety of different workflows that can really help us get deeper into your personas, their jobs to be done.
That's really, really important for us because we really want to think about what are those sets.
of questions that are really important, are higher volume, are going to drive engagement, and how can we create the best answers to those questions?
And that can start to help us inform what we think about in terms of our strategy.
And so in the early phases of starting to dial this in with our customers, this can just help lay the foundation for what we want to do from a content strategy standpoint.
From there, kickoff process, I'm sure we've all been on a variety of different kickoffs, similar model.
Of course, we have our own bit of a flavor to how we approach that.
But, you know, we're just making sure we understand your business objectives, personas at a very deep level.

From there, what we start to do is develop a rich set of topical opportunities that we find doing a set of content gap analyses, going through that jobs-to-be-done framework, understanding those questions at a deep level, and starting to build what we call our content OS.
I'm happy to show that for you.
But I think in this case, this is probably something.
Where we probably to a degree follow that, but would have to tweak the approach a little bit because it skews a bit more.
I'm thinking about programmatic and editorial ideas.

How do we start to calibrate what actually makes sense for these outputs from the workflows with your team?
Here, we're trying to make sure we have the right tone, structure, et cetera.
Again, what this can look like, depending on how custom the engagement is, can be a bit different.
But more so, it's trying to understand the patterns so we can learn as fast as possible.
How do we, you know, every workflow has an output, right?
With the inputs we put into those workflows, as much as we can refine and tweak them and start to find consistent outputs, that just helps us move faster and faster to generate high-quality content.
So, think of it as, like, we're trying to develop a set of hypotheses based off of your feedback that we
And then say, okay, based off what they're saying, maybe if we do this, we tweak our workflow, prompt in a different way, whatever that may be, can we check that box?
And if we can, that just lets us move much faster.
If not, we typically bring in writers that we train to help enable us and block us as that continues to evolve.
So, you know, I can continue to go in here, but realizing this is a little bit custom, and again, this tends to skew a little bit more towards editorial programmatic.
You know, don't want to go too into the weeds and realize we only have a handful of minutes left, but I can at least show you a little bit of kind of what this looks like in the back end.
And, you know, here for us, like we're building a set of different workflows, again, that we think about breaking down the jobs to be done of our content teams.
So, you know, here is one that is, you know, the more basic side, but quite powerful.
And just illustrating a little bit of how we break these.

And so, you know, as we think about a topic, you know, one of the key inputs to our early stages of our workflows to generate content is let's understand, let's feed in context about your personal business, your business, any other sort of like brand tone guidelines we've refined in other workflows and collaboration with you.

And then with the topic, if you were just someone who's sitting down and starting to think about developing the piece of content, you need to think about start doing that research and thinking of like, who are these people?
What kind of questions do they have?
And so that's what this workflow like this is built to do.
It just helps us start to say, OK, you know, input all that rich information of who these people are.
What kind of questions do they have?
How can we generate even further deeper questions doing research across a variety of different mediums, leveraging tools such as perplexity, you dot com, et cetera.
And then.
Continue to do deep research on those questions, start to process citations, start to bring that together.
And the output of that is, I'll just show you a very quick example.
Here is one from our workflow.
This is, it looks to be related to AI technologies for, related to HVAC.
And so here, like, you know, generating an initial set of questions and, you know, what are the most effective AI technologies available for, sorry, this is getting in my way here.
Let's move this out of way.
For optimizing sales processes for the HVAC industry and just continuing to develop that rich set of questions.
And then the output of that, I know this isn't the most amazing format right now, certainly looks a little bit different when we're sharing this.
But the output of that format is, like, quite a thorough breakdown and brief.
Of the answers to those questions that then help us feed in into creating that high quality content that is informed by those.
Happy to, you know, there's so many layers to this.
realize, you know, going super, super fast here, you know, just wanted to try to like give you a little bit of a flavor of how some of this looks.
But realize as well, you know, this is a pretty unique, you know, kind of opportunity.
So I'm happy to answer any clarifying questions from what I just ran through.
But it is, you know, is this something that it sounds like it's a little bit different than what you anticipated?
But I'm curious, you know, initial thoughts.
I think it's, I mean, for me at least, and I got to jump in two or three minutes, but I think it's really interesting.
I think it would be helpful for us maybe if you guys kind of, you know, thought about what we've told you today and came back to us with just some.
Ideas on how we could work together.
Chris can maybe send you a list of like 20 or 30 of our sites that maybe we'd want to focus on and just see if you think there's something there that could work.
And then ultimately, like, you know, what would it cost and what, you know, what kind of objectives do you think we could collectively come up with?
Because, yeah, I think it could be interesting.
I actually think you're, you know, the takeaway for me is you guys are deeply passionate.
You workflows.
And I think your focus on workflows is important to us because it makes it more scalable across, you know, a large footprint that we have.
And that's where we're struggling is like we're we're not too bad at this on a small scale.
Like we can do a couple of sites ourselves.
But, you know, to scale it to twelve hundred communities would be a little daunting.
So, um, the commit.
Around workflows makes it interesting.
Cool.
Cool.
Yeah, happy to find some time to dig in deeper, just for context, catching us at a little bit of an interesting time.
Marcel's on vacation this week.
I'm going on vacation next week, but I'm sure we can find a time where we can all come together and chat.
Chris, you could share some of the context, would be great on those websites.
Additionally, if you have it, just ready and easy to pass.

If there's anything you have that you could share around what you've done to start to develop that engagement today, any sort of tactics and context, just to make sure I can kind of get my head around and start to connect the dots a little bit.
If helpful, happy to find time later this week just to have a quick chat on that.
Yeah, maybe you and I could take it offline, Kyle, and I can show you some stuff.
Yeah, that sounds good.
Actually, if you have a moment, you want to just get that on the calendar right now?
Sure.
Cool.
And then what I'm going to do is also start a Slack channel, so that way we can streamline the communication there as well.
Oh, cool.
It was great to meet you guys.
I'm going to check out here.
Thank you.
All right.
Thanks, Rob.
Great meeting you.
Take care.
All right.
So on my end, pretty...
Actually, let me make sure I'm looking at the right way.
Yep.
Thursday's pretty open for me between 10 and 1 p.m.
Pacific.
Any chance that we're...
Yeah, 10 p.m.
Pacific's fine.
Sorry, I didn't catch it.
At what time at Pacific?
10 or 10.30 Pacific.
Totally fine.
Okay, cool, cool, cool.
I will send that over in just a moment.
And yeah, looking forward to digging in a little bit more, Chris.
Yeah, I'll send you some domains just so you can get a flavor for the types of communities.
We run a common platform across all these different sites.
So it's common software, common technology.
It's just separate domain names, obviously separate content, separate community members that...
But even if you just wanted to get a general feel for one of our forums, like Ben's World, Ben'sWorld.org, Ben'sWorld.org is sort of one of our classic, it's a classic car forum, right?
It's just we modernize them and, you know, provide that common software platform for them.
And so, yeah, and Rob mentioned, like, the use case we have in mind is how could we leverage, you know, AI tools to, you know, accelerate content production within the community, right?
But in a way that doesn't lose kind of the authenticity, which makes these places, like, kind of really special.
It really taps into people's true life experiences and expertise.
So whether it's prompting more, you know, questions, whether it's, you know, answering or reviving old threads or, you know, these types of ideas, I know it's not necessarily right in the wheelhouse.
But at the end of the day, content is content, right?
It's just a question of what's it being used for.
And in our case, it's knowledge sharing right across a given subject matter.
So, you know, take Ben's World and then multiply that by 1200.
That's kind of what we've got as a business.
We reach about 120 million users a month right across our platform.
So we've got a lot of scale, over 2 billion posts of content as well.
Wow.
So like from a data asset perspective, even a training perspective, like we're sitting on a, we think a very valuable data asset.
So maybe there's some ways for us to collaborate there as well, right?
Yeah, yeah.
And if we're not even, you know, we've just scratched the surface, I'm sure, of getting to know about each other's companies.
So there could be, there could be multiple ways.
Cool.
Love it.
Yeah, no, super excited to learn more.
I'll send that time out for 10 a.m.
Pacific on Thursday.
Yeah, we'll start digging into Ben's world in the meantime.
Okay.
Awesome.
All right, Chris.
Thanks for so much for having me.
Likewise, Chris.
See you guys.
See you.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Explore potential collaboration between VerticalScope and GrowthX for AI-driven content generation and community engagement](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=5.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [VerticalScope seeks to leverage AI for increasing content velocity and quality in user-generated forums, particularly in automotive communities](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)
  - [GrowthX specializes in AI-powered content workflows, primarily for editorial and programmatic content, but is open to exploring UGC applications](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1112.0)
  - [Both parties expressed interest in further discussions to explore customized solutions for VerticalScope's unique needs](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1664.0)
  - [Follow-up meeting scheduled for Thursday at 10am Pacific to dive deeper into VerticalScope's specific use cases and communities](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1786.0)

## Topics

### VerticalScope's Business and Challenges

  - [Operates 1200+ enthusiast community forums across various niches](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1938.0)
  - [Reaches \~120 million users/month with 2 billion+ posts of content](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1941.0)
  - [Facing challenges in user engagement post-COVID and Google algorithm changes](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=369.0)
  - [Seeking ways to increase content velocity, quality, and user revisits, especially in automotive forums](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=323.0)

### GrowthX's Capabilities and Approach

  - [Specializes in AI-powered content workflows for editorial and programmatic content](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=568.0)
  - [Uses a "Content OS" approach to break down content creation into efficient workflows](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=568.0)
  - [Leverages AI to accelerate research, question generation, and content structuring](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=744.0)
  - [Customizes solutions based on client needs and objectives](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1646.0)

### Potential Collaboration Areas

  - [Exploring AI applications for user-generated content in forum settings](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1905.0)
  - [Increasing post frequency, quality, and SEO-friendliness](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=341.0)
  - [Leveraging VerticalScope's extensive data assets for potential AI training](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1947.0)
  - [Scaling content generation across VerticalScope's large network of communities](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1931.0)

### Next Steps

  - [Chris (VerticalScope) to send list of 20-30 focus sites and additional context](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1842.0)
  - [Kyle (GrowthX) to review VerticalScope's sites, particularly Ben'sWorld.org](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1860.0)
  - [Follow-up meeting scheduled for Thursday at 10am Pacific to discuss specific use cases and potential solutions](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1786.0)
  - [GrowthX to consider and propose ideas for collaboration, including potential objectives and cost estimates](https://fathom.video/share/-wvH7EYaiS8RGF_KJtnfEs5TtsFhxKpx?tab=summary&timestamp=1652.0)
